coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon dots as antibiotics sensor and fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Mathew, Sneha
John, Bony K.
Mathew, Jincy
Korah, Binila K.
Mathew, Dr. Beena
coredata.dc:description: Nearly all antibiotics cause significant health issues and environmental risks due to their unrestricted intake and delayed consumption. So, their detection and removal are of great concern. In this work, a novel Coleus zeylanicus leaves-derived nitrogen doped carbon dot (NCDs) based sensor was developed for the detection of antibiotics. The NCDs were synthesized via cost effective hydrothermal method. The NCDs with amorphous nature have an average size of 2.59 nm and a surface charge of -30.89 mV and exhibited excellent stability towards various experimental conditions. The NCDs worked as a fluorescent probe for levofloxacin (LEV) detection and an electrochemical sensing probe for the detection of amoxicillin (AMX). The fluorescence of NCDs was enhanced by LEV, and the limit of detection is obtained as 0.06 µM. The NCDs detected AMX with a LOD of 0.03 nM. Additionally, the effectiveness of NCDs in sensing levofloxacin and amoxicillin was confirmed in spiked water samples. Also, the role of NCDs as fluorescent ink was explored. In light of the findings of this study, it could be possible to find new ways to address environmental issues using carbon dots from sustainable sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Coleus zeylanicus
Levofloxacin
Amoxicillin
Fluorescent ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical characteristics of the sensing probe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Senior Research Fellowship (SRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra, (b) wavelength study, (c) FT-IR spectrum, and (d) zeta potential of NCDs.
XPS (a) full scan, high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s.
(a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM, (c) particle size distribution, and (d) DLS of carbon dots.
(a) XRD pattern, and (b) Raman spectrum of NCDs.
Study of the effect of (a) photo irradiation, (b) ionic concentration, (c) pH, and (d) time on the photostability of NCDs.
(a) Selectivity study, (b) effect of concentration of LEV, (c) plot of concentration of LEV against F/F0, and (d) interference study (where X is all interferents taken).
(a) CV runs for the detection of amoxicillin, effect of (b) pH, (c) scan rate, and (d) selectivity study.
(a) DPV, (b) plot of concentration vs. current, (c) amperometric response, and (d) impedance spectrum.
Electrochemical detection of amoxicillin in water samples.
Images showing fluorescent ink properties of NCDs.
Scheme for the synthesis and applications of NCDs.
Mechanism of sensing of LEV by NCDs.
Electrochemical sensing mechanism of amoxicillin by NCD/GCE.
Detection of LEV in water samples through fluorescence method.

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon dots as antibiotics sensor and fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala 686560, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam, Kerala 686560, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nearly all antibiotics cause significant health issues and environmental risks due to their unrestricted intake and delayed consumption. So, their detection and removal are of great concern. In this work, a novel Coleus zeylanicus leaves-derived nitrogen doped carbon dot (NCDs) based sensor was developed for the detection of antibiotics. The NCDs were synthesized via cost effective hydrothermal method. The NCDs with amorphous nature have an average size of 2.59 nm and a surface charge of -30.89 mV and exhibited excellent stability towards various experimental conditions. The NCDs worked as a fluorescent probe for levofloxacin (LEV) detection and an electrochemical sensing probe for the detection of amoxicillin (AMX). The fluorescence of NCDs was enhanced by LEV, and the limit of detection is obtained as 0.06 µM. The NCDs detected AMX with a LOD of 0.03 nM. Additionally, the effectiveness of NCDs in sensing levofloxacin and amoxicillin was confirmed in spiked water samples. Also, the role of NCDs as fluorescent ink was explored. In light of the findings of this study, it could be possible to find new ways to address environmental issues using carbon dots from sustainable sources.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of Penicillin, antibiotics have played a significant role in human and animal life. They are well known around the world as antibacterial medicine that can be used to cure illness and promote animal growth [1]. However, their misuse is one of the major problems society is currently facing. The majority of the antibiotics used by humans and animals would be eliminated as their original medication or their metabolites. As a result, they are excreted through human or animal excreta and pollute water [2–4]. The introduction of antibiotics to the environment harmed not only humans but also the entire ecological system. This can result in the production of antibiotic-resistant bacteria that are harmful to life [5,6]. Moreover, their potential to accumulate in food chains and cause many hazardous symptoms like organ damage and hearing loss, antibiotics have negative effects, even at trace doses, on human health [7]. Additionally, these residues are released into rivers and possibly the oceans through wastewater after bio-metabolism, given that it is very water soluble and has limited biodegradability [8,9]. Thus, their detection is very important.

In recent years, experts have focused on using various analytical approaches to quantitative/ qualitative identification of antibiotics. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], Gas chromatography coupled mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [11], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [12], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [13], etc. however, several barriers including expensive time and equipment limit the use of chromatography as a determination technique. These techniques typically have some limitations, such as high cost, time-consuming sample pre-treatment, and expensive instrumentation, which render them unsuitable for routinely monitoring the environment and food safety [14]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop new methods for antibiotic detection that are highly selective and sensitive.

Due to their sensitivity, fast performance, usefulness, and inexpensive nature, fluorescence, and electrochemical methods are widely adopted to detect antibiotics. Additionally, electrochemical sensors depend on the electrical signal produced by the electrocatalytic reaction between analytes and electrodes. This significantly affects the test performance of the electrochemical response [15]. Therefore, the creation of an electrochemical sensor that is quick, trustworthy, and sensitive will encourage the use of electrochemical methods for antibiotic detection. Among the different fluorescent and electrochemical sensors, recently developed carbon-based fluorescent nanostructures called carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention. Recent advancements in electrochemical sensing have been made by carbon dots, a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, in part because of its great solubility, low toxicity, simplicity of modification, modest size effect, and strong electrochemical activity [15]. Also, due to their high electivity, affordability and ease of use CD-based fluorescence sensing finds extensive applications [16]. Carbon dots have a lot of potential in various applications, including bioimaging, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, nanoprobes, and biomedicine, due to their ease of synthesis, excellent optical stability surface functionalization, and superior biocompatibility [17–20]. The primary benefit of CDs over other quantum dots is their easy, affordable, and widespread one-step synthesis from a variety of carbon-containing sources [21]. Here we suggest an alternative method for using this large biomass as a cheap raw material for the synthesis of carbon dots.

The fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from Coleus zeylanicus for the first time. Coleus zeylanicus is a small, tender herb with several branches with a fragrance and blue flowers. It has antibacterial, antipyretic, antidiabetic, and hepato-protective activities. It is frequently used for treating vomiting, diarrhea, leukoderma, etc. [22,23]. In this work, a green approach for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) for the detection of antibiotics was described. The NCDs were prepared via hydrothermal method from Coleus zeylanicus as the novel carbon source and triethylene tetraamine as the nitrogen source. The NCDs acted as a fluorescent sensor for levofloxacin via a fluorescent enhancement mechanism, and electrochemically it detected amoxicillin with very low detection limits. The results of this work indicated NCDs exhibited excellent analytical performance both in fluorescent and electrochemical methods for antibiotics detection. Moreover, we also extended its application to the field of fluorescent ink (Scheme 1 ).

The reagents used for the study include Coleus zeylanicus leaves, monosodium phosphate, disodium phosphate (Alfa Aesar), levofloxacin, amoxicillin (Sigma-Aldrich), and other antibiotics and reagents. All the reagents used here were purchased from reputed sources and of standard grades and used without further purification. Double distilled water was used for the entire experiment.

UV-vis. absorption spectrum was recorded using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrum was studied using Shimadzu RF-6000 spectroflurophotometer. FT-IR analysis was carried out on a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 400 spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a JOEL JEM-2100 microscope, and the XRD patterns were recorded using a Bruker D8 Discover with Cu Kα radiation. XPS data were collected from the Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer. The DLS and zeta potential measurements were done on a Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer. Electrochemical studies were conducted on a Biologic SP-300 electrochemical workstation.

The NCDs were prepared via hydrothermal method from Coleus zeylanicus leaves as the carbon source and triethylene tetraamine as the nitrogen source. Initially, the reactants were mixed in the ratio 1:2 (C:N) and placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 180°C for 10 h. Once the reaction was complete, it was allowed to cool, and the obtained suspension was centrifuged, filtered, and stored at 4°C for further use.

The sensing of LEV through the fluorescence method was carried out in the presence of NCDs at 340 nm excitation. The NCDs solution was mixed with 0.001N different antibiotics solution. The mixture was sonicated and the fluorescence spectra was recorded. For interference studies different analytes were added to the NCD+ LEV mixture and fluorescence spectra was recorded at 340 nm.

For the electrochemical detection of amoxicillin glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode, Pt wire as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The electrochemical experiments were carried out on a GCE coated with NCDs at a pH 7.
The NCDs were directly injected into an empty pen refill and used directly without any pre-treatments for drawing and writing. The images were illuminated under a UV lamp, and photographs were taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics of the sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: UV-vis. absorption spectra of the NCDs showed three peaks at 210, 275, and 335 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of C=C, C=N and n-π* transition of C=O groups respectively [24,25]. The NCDs solution appears brown under daylight, whereas it exhibits blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1 a). The fluorescence spectra of NCDs were examined at different excitation wavelengths from 320-400 nm. Maximum emission intensity was obtained at 460 nm on exciting at 340 nm. In the fluorescence spectra, a red shift was observed in the range 380-400 nm (Fig. 1b). The functional groups present in NCDs act as the excitation energy traps and lead to the diverse fluorescent properties by introducing various defects on the surface throughout the hydrothermal carbonization process, which is likely the origin of the excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviour [26].

In addition, the chemical makeup of NCDs was analyzed by FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The characteristic absorption band around 3380-3225 cm−1 corresponds to O-H/ N-H stretching vibrations. The absorption band at 1638, 1366, and 1156 cm−1 corresponds to C=O, C-N, and C-O stretching (Fig. 1c) [27]. The zeta potential measurements reveal a significant negative charge of 30.89 mV, indicating the presence of oxidized groups on the surface of NCDs (Fig. 1d). A weak band in the range 2200 - 2000 cm−1 indicated the presence of nitrile group [28]. The XPS analysis confirms the presence of 68.14 % of C 1s, 17.76 % of O 1s, and 14.10 % of N 1s (Fig. 2 a). When high-resolution C 1s spectra are closely observed 3 peaks may be deconvoluted, which may be attributed to C=C/C-C, C-O/ C-N, and C=O corresponding to 284.81, 285.40, and 287.39 eV (Fig. 2b) [29]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra show peaks at 529.98 and 530.97 eV corresponding to C=O and C-OH/ C-O-C bonds (Fig. 2c). The N 1s high-resolution spectra corresponding to different chemicals environment for C-N-C and N-H were obtained at 398.08 and 399.84 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d) [30]. The presence of -OH, -NH, -COOH groups were confirmed by both FT-IR and XPS analysis. These groups contribute to the stability, hydrophilicity, and fluorescence property of the carbon dots [31].

The quantum yield (QY) of the NCDs was measured as 16.9 % by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity to that of the reference quinine sulfate (54 %) in 0.1 M H2SO4. Quantum yield is calculated using the formula
(1)
Q
Y S
= Q
Y R
(
A R
/
A S
)
(
I S
/
I R
)
(
η S
/
η R
)
2
Where R and S are the reference and sample, A - absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I - the integrated fluorescence intensity, and ƞ - refractive index of the solvent.

The size and morphology of the NCDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. TEM image indicates that the particles are spherical in shape with size distribution in the range of 1.5-4.5 nm with an average size of 2.59 nm (Fig. 3 a, c). The interplanar distance of 0.24 nm confirms the (002) graphitic carbon plane (Fig. 3b inset). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were carried out to find out the hydrodynamic diameter of the NCDs. The average hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs was 4 nm (Fig. 3d) which is very close to the TEM data. The XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 22.96°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 4 a) [32,33]. Raman spectrum gives peaks at 1341 and 1562 cm−1 corresponding to D (sp3 carbon) and G (sp2 carbon) bands, respectively (Fig. 4b).

For any fluorometric probe, fluorescence stability is an important parameter. Here, the stability is analyzed by changing ambient conditions like photo irradiation, ionic concentration, and pH. The photostability of the NCDs was analyzed by continuous irradiation of UV light for 30 min. The fluorescent intensity remains almost the same during this time confirming the photostability of NCDs (Fig. 5 a). The stability in the ionic environment was tested in KCl solutions having different concentrations (0 to 2 M), and no change in fluorescence intensity was noted (Fig. 5b). The effect of pH (3 to 10) on the fluorescent intensity of NCDs was studied. The fluorescence intensity was maximum at pH 4 buffer solution (Fig. 5c). To check the room temperature stability, the NCDs were kept at room temperature for 30 days, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded every 5 days. Surprisingly the NCDs exhibited appreciable stability (Fig. 5d). These results of the stability studies indicated that the developed NCDs sensor has exceptional stability in different ionic environments, UV light, pH, and room temperature.

The synthesized NCDs were used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of levofloxacin (LEV). To analyze the response of NCDs towards levofloxacin, the effect of other antibiotics like ciprofloxacin (CIP), amoxicillin (AMX), tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DOX), cloxacillin (CLOXA), kanamycin (KAN), and vancomycin (VAN) were taken. It was observed that only LEV changes the fluorescent intensity of NCDs significantly. An enhancement in fluorescent intensity was observed upon adding LEV (Fig. 6 a). Under optimized conditions, different concentrations of LEV were added to the NCDs solution, and the fluorescent intensity was measured at 340 nm excitation. It was noted that fluorescent intensity gradually increased with an increase in LEV concentration (Fig. 6b). From the plot of the concentration of LEV against F/F0, it was observed that the fluorescence emission intensity was increased over a fixed concentration range of LEV, which suggests that the NCDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of LEV (Fig. 6c). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula
(2)
LOD = 3 σ / m
Where σ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of liner fit. The LOD is calculated as low as 0.06 µM in the linear range of 0-1µM. Table S1 provides some of the previously reported results of LEV detection for comparison. The effect of other interfering species on LEV detection was also investigated. For this CIP, AMX, TC, DOX, CLOXA, KAN, VAN, H+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ were added to the NCD+ LEV mixture. It was noted that the fluorescent intensity remained almost the same in the presence of other potential interferants (Fig. 6d), which opens the door for its practical applications. To further evaluate the practical application of the sensing probe real sample analysis was carried out. Two groups of water samples were taken and filtered to remove any impurities, spiked with a definite quantity of LEV, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. Detection rates of 98.40-99.93 % were obtained for tap water and 99.70-99.78 % for river water.

The fluorescence intensity of NCDs remarkably enhanced with LEV, and linear relation in fluorescent intensity was observed with an increase in LEV concentration (Fig. 6b). The fluorescence enhancement mechanism may be due to hydrogen bonding interaction (Scheme 2 ). The surface of NCDs is rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which facilitate water solubility and hydrogen bond interaction with LEV, which contains C-O, C-F, and C-N groups. The zeta potential of NCDs was measured as -24.68 mV in the presence of LEV (Fig. S1). The interaction of NCDs with LEV causes this decrease in negative zeta potential value. Substantial hydrogen bonding interaction occurs between NCDs and LEV when they come close to each other resulting in fluorescence enhancement [34]. The enhanced fluorescence at acidic pH indicates the presence of hydrogen bonding interaction (Fig. S2). This is attributed to the partial protonation of nitrogen and oxygen atoms, which enhances the potential of hydrogen bonding between the NCDs and levofloxacin [35]. Another reason for the fluorescent enhancement is the electrostatic repulsion between the NCDs and the LEV adsorbed on its surface, which might increase the distance between the NCDs and minimizes nonradiative recombination [36,37]. As a result, the molecule spent more time in the excited state. Which is evident from the lifetime measurements. The life time of NCDS increased from 5.68 to 5.75 ns with the addition of LEV (Fig. S3).

Before modification, the GCE was polished with alumina slurry and dried. The NCD-modified GCE was prepared by casting NCD mixed with Nafion on the GCE surface, and the solvent was evaporated. To examine the electrochemical response of various electrodes CV was recorded in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Fig. 7 a represents the cyclic voltammetric curves for the electrochemical detection of amoxicillin at the optimized conditions (scan rate 50 mV and pH 7). There is no pronounced peak in the bare GCE, which implies that there cannot be any electrochemical reactions without carbon dots. Compared to the NCD/GCE, the AMX/NCD/GCE produced an enlarged current response. The anodic peak current of AMX/NCD/GCE is about 7.3 times larger than that of bare GCE, whereas it is 5.5 times higher than the NCD/GCE. This may be due to the enhanced surface area on coating the electrode surface with NCDs.

The effect of pH on amoxicillin sensing by the sensor was evaluated in the potential range of -0.2 to 0.6. Among the different pH (3 - 10) tested, the maximum current response was obtained at pH 7, indicating the optimum pH for amoxicillin sensing (Fig. 7b). The scan rate study from 50-100 mV were done and found that with increase in scan rate the current response also increased linearly (Fig. 7c). The selectivity of the NCD-modified GCE towards different antibiotics was studied, and among them, only amoxicillin exhibited a substantial current response (Fig. 7d). The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was carried out in the presence of an increasing concentration of amoxicillin (Fig. 8 a). With the increase in the concentration of amoxicillin, the current also increased linearly (Fig. 8b). The calculated detection limit was 0.03 nM. The detection potential, sensitivity, detection limit, and linear range of the NCD/GCE amoxicillin sensor were compared to those of other amoxicillin sensors described in recent literature. The developed sensor outperforms many of the reported sensors (Table S2). The electrochemical performance of the sensor was further analyzed by amperometric current-time responses by continuous addition of amoxicillin (Fig. 8c). Apparently, the current response increased with an increase in amoxicillin concentration which suggests that the electrode has a good response time and a general stable performance. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis is a crucial field for evaluating the conductivity and resistance of the developed electrode material [38]. The outcomes of EIS are depicted using Nyquist plots. The larger semicircle radius of bare GCE than NCD/GCE in the Nyquist plot indicates higher charge transfer resistance of bare GCE and lower resistance for NCD/GCE (Fig. 8d).

By using the conventional addition method, we added a certain concentration of AMX to tap and river water in order to evaluate the practicability of the developed sensor. The results reported detection rates in the range of 99.20 % to 100.18 % (Fig. 10 ). Therefore, the NCD-modified electrode has great potential in practical applications.

In conclusion, blue fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized via a green synthesis method from biomass. The successful formation of the NCDs was confirmed via different characterization techniques such as UV-vis., FT-IR, XPS, TEM, XRD, DLS, and zeta potential. It was found that the optical response of the proposed nanosensor was stable in saline solution, pH variation, UV exposure, and time. The fabricated carbon dots were made used to develop a sensing platform to detect levofloxacin in both standard and real samples. These NCDs showed selective fluorescence enhancement in the presence of levofloxacin and a lower detection limit of 0.06 µM. Glassy carbon electrode modified with NCDs to detect amoxicillin selectively. The developed sensor acted as a dual sensor for antibiotics. Furthermore, the application of NCDs as fluorescent ink was studied. These results provide a concise direction for designing stable, accessible, and cost-effective carbon dots for multi-purpose applications.

Sneha Mathew: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Bony K. John: Data curation, Visualization. Jincy Mathew: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Binila K. Korah: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Dr. Beena Mathew: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Greatly acknowledges the financial assistance received from the University Grants Commission (UGC) in the form of Senior Research Fellowship (SRF).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.136422.

Sci. Total Environ.
